title: How Cinemas Taught Early Movie-Goers the Rules & Etiquette for Watching Films (1912): No Whistling, Standing or Wearing Big Hats
description:
in Film |   	July 13th, 2026  Leave a Comment
	
			
I admit, I some¬≠times pay a pre¬≠mi¬≠um at a cer¬≠tain din¬≠ner the¬≠ater chain with a lob¬≠by-slash-b
body:
in Film |   	July 13th, 2026  Leave a Comment
	
			
I admit, I some¬≠times pay a pre¬≠mi¬≠um at a cer¬≠tain din¬≠ner the¬≠ater chain with a lob¬≠by-slash-bar designed to look like clas¬≠sic indie video stores of yore. It‚Äôs not only the padded reclin¬≠ers and half-decent grub that keeps me com¬≠ing back. Nope, it‚Äôs the rules. Print¬≠ed on the menu are a list of dis¬≠rup¬≠tive behav¬≠iors that will get you uncer¬≠e¬≠mo¬≠ni¬≠ous¬≠ly tossed out‚ÄĒno refunds and no back¬≠sies.
I‚Äôve nev¬≠er seen it hap¬≠pen. Giv¬≠en what peo¬≠ple put down for tick¬≠ets, din¬≠ner, drinks, and/or a babysit¬≠ter, it‚Äôs unlike¬≠ly many risk blow¬≠ing the evening. But know¬≠ing that the the¬≠ater takes silence seri¬≠ous¬≠ly brings seri¬≠ous movie¬≠go¬≠ers peace of mind. What is a movie, after all, with¬≠out the all-impor¬≠tant dia¬≠logue, music, and sound cues?

Well, it‚Äôs silent film. And even then, when movies were sound-tracked with live accom¬≠pa¬≠ni¬≠ment and dia¬≠logue appeared on title cards, peo¬≠ple wor¬≠ried very much about dis¬≠trac¬≠tions. It just so hap¬≠pens that talk¬≠ing and tex¬≠ting (obvi¬≠ous¬≠ly) were the least of ear¬≠ly audience‚Äôs con¬≠cerns.
For one thing, the cin¬≠e¬≠ma was a place where class¬≠es, races, sex¬≠es, and ages ‚Äúmixed much more freely than had been Vic¬≠to¬≠ri¬≠an cus¬≠tom,‚ÄĚ notes Rebec¬≠ca Onion at Slate. There were the usu¬≠al con¬≠cerns about cor¬≠rup¬≠tion of the ‚Äúdel¬≠i¬≠cate sen¬≠si¬≠bil¬≠i¬≠ties‚ÄĚ of ladies.

‚ÄúBut female cin¬≠e¬≠ma-goers were just as like¬≠ly to be seen as a prob¬≠lem,‚ÄĚ writes Onion, ‚Äúgiv¬≠en their sup¬≠posed propen¬≠si¬≠ty for wear¬≠ing big hats and chat¬≠ting.‚ÄĚ The melt¬≠ing pot demo¬≠graph¬≠ic of the nick¬≠elodeon could be exhil¬≠a¬≠rat¬≠ing, and audi¬≠ence mem¬≠bers found they some¬≠times lost their inhi¬≠bi¬≠tions. ‚ÄúSome¬≠how you enter into the spir¬≠it of the thing,‚ÄĚ observed author W.W. Win¬≠ters in 1910. ‚ÄúDon‚Äôt you slip away from your¬≠self, lose your ret¬≠i¬≠cence, reserve, pride, and a few oth¬≠er things?‚ÄĚ

These days we‚Äôre accus¬≠tomed to cram¬≠ming in elbow-to-elbow next to any¬≠one and every¬≠one, and we most¬≠ly heed the onscreen cajol¬≠ing to put our phones away and keep qui¬≠et, even when we aren‚Äôt in spe¬≠cial¬≠ty bou¬≠tique chains or local art¬≠house the¬≠aters. Then again, if cer¬≠tain behav¬≠iors weren‚Äôt an issue, there wouldn‚Äôt be ads pro¬≠hibit¬≠ing them.

Enor¬≠mous hats and applause (and applause with things oth¬≠er than hands) may be relics of cinema‚Äôs infan¬≠cy. But swap out those admo¬≠ni¬≠tions for oth¬≠ers of the smart¬≠phone vari¬≠ety and these lantern slides instruct¬≠ing view¬≠ers in 1912 about prop¬≠er movie the¬≠ater eti¬≠quette don‚Äôt look so dif¬≠fer¬≠ent from today‚Ä¶ sort of.

We might want for inter¬≠mis¬≠sions to return, espe¬≠cial¬≠ly after the two-hour mark, and wouldn‚Äôt it be nice if, instead of keep¬≠ing us in our seats for post-cred¬≠it scenes, big block¬≠buster movies just said ‚ÄúGood Night‚ÄĚ? See more of these delight¬≠ful pub¬≠lic ser¬≠vice announce¬≠ments from 1912 nick¬≠elodeons at Back Sto¬≠ry Radio.

via Slate
Note: An ear¬≠li¬≠er ver¬≠sion of this post appeared on our site in 2019.
Relat¬≠ed Con¬≠tent:
The Art of Cre¬≠at¬≠ing Spe¬≠cial Effects in Silent Movies: Inge¬≠nu¬≠ity Before the Age of CGI
Enjoy the Great¬≠est Silent Films Ever Made in Our Col¬≠lec¬≠tion of 101 Free Silent Films Online
The Char¬≠lie Chap¬≠lin Archive Opens, Putting Online 30,000 Pho¬≠tos & Doc¬≠u¬≠ments from the Life of the Icon¬≠ic Film Star
Josh Jones is a writer and musi¬≠cian based in Durham, NC.

title: Tatlin‚Äôs Tower, One of the Most Ambitious Buildings That Was Never Built
description:
in Architecture, Art, History |   	July 13th, 2026  Leave a Comment
	
			

	

It‚Äôs no small project to found a new soci¬≠ety, espe¬≠cial¬≠ly when you‚Äôr
body:
in Architecture, Art, History |   	July 13th, 2026  Leave a Comment
	
			

	

It‚Äôs no small project to found a new soci¬≠ety, espe¬≠cial¬≠ly when you‚Äôre doing it on the scale of a place like Rus¬≠sia. Apart from the con¬≠sid¬≠er¬≠able prac¬≠ti¬≠cal chal¬≠lenges it entails, there‚Äôs also the need for sym¬≠bols bold enough to rep¬≠re¬≠sent the under¬≠ly¬≠ing ide¬≠al. The avant-garde artist Vladimir Tatlin took it upon him¬≠self to cre¬≠ate just such a sym¬≠bol in the years after the Russ¬≠ian Rev¬≠o¬≠lu¬≠tion. The result is offi¬≠cial¬≠ly known as the Mon¬≠u¬≠ment to the Third Inter¬≠na¬≠tion¬≠al, named for the orga¬≠ni¬≠za¬≠tion tasked with the pro¬≠mo¬≠tion of world com¬≠mu¬≠nism (often abbre¬≠vi¬≠at¬≠ed to Com¬≠intern). But it‚Äôs more com¬≠mon¬≠ly referred to as Tatlin‚Äôs Tow¬≠er, per¬≠haps in trib¬≠ute to the artist‚Äôs par¬≠tic¬≠u¬≠lar vision ‚ÄĒ one too ambi¬≠tious for its real-life con¬≠struc¬≠tion even to begin.

‚ÄúAs part of a large-scale pro¬≠gram to replace old czarist mon¬≠u¬≠ments with mon¬≠u¬≠ments to the rev¬≠o¬≠lu¬≠tion, the huge struc¬≠ture was both a sym¬≠bol¬≠ic sculp¬≠ture and func¬≠tion¬≠al archi¬≠tec¬≠ture,‚ÄĚ write Smarthis¬≠to¬≠ry‚Äôs¬†Charles Cramer and Kim Grant. ‚ÄúDesigned to strad¬≠dle the Neva riv¬≠er in St. Peters¬≠burg, the 1300 foot (400 meter) iron and glass Mon¬≠u¬≠ment would sur¬≠pass Paris‚Äôs Eif¬≠fel Tow¬≠er in both scale and com¬≠plex¬≠i¬≠ty.‚ÄĚ
Indeed, it would stand taller than the yet-to-be-con¬≠struct¬≠ed Empire State Build¬≠ing, at least if you don‚Äôt count its anten¬≠na. Con¬≠sist¬≠ing of ‚Äúa con¬≠tract¬≠ing dou¬≠ble helix that spi¬≠rals upward, sup¬≠port¬≠ed by a huge diag¬≠o¬≠nal gird¬≠er,‚ÄĚ Tatlin‚Äôs Tow¬≠er would con¬≠tain four sub-struc¬≠tures, each rotat¬≠ing at a dif¬≠fer¬≠ent speed.


	

Yes, rotat¬≠ing, and ‚Äúcom¬≠plet¬≠ing a full rev¬≠o¬≠lu¬≠tion in accor¬≠dance with the impor¬≠tance of the insti¬≠tu¬≠tions con¬≠duct¬≠ing their busi¬≠ness on the inside,‚ÄĚ as Tim Brinkof writes at Big Think. ‚ÄúThe cube that con¬≠tains the leg¬≠is¬≠la¬≠ture would have com¬≠plet¬≠ed a full rota¬≠tion once per year. The pyra¬≠mid above, hous¬≠ing the offices of par¬≠ty exec¬≠u¬≠tives, would have need¬≠ed a month. The infor¬≠ma¬≠tion cen¬≠ter, locat¬≠ed at the very peak, would have rotat¬≠ed once a day, offer¬≠ing a 360-degree view of Pet¬≠ro¬≠grad,‚ÄĚ as St. Peters¬≠burg was known in 1920. (It would be re-named Leningrad in 1924 before going back to St. Peters¬≠burg in 1991, after the end of the Sovi¬≠et era.) You can learn more about how it all worked from the Archi¬≠tec¬≠ture Enthu¬≠si¬≠ast video at the top of the post, and the one from Side¬≠pro¬≠jects just above.

‚ÄúTatlin‚Äôs Tow¬≠er was designed dur¬≠ing a time when Com¬≠mu¬≠nist rule was still nascent and par¬≠ty lead¬≠ers sought to estab¬≠lish a new and dis¬≠tinct¬≠ly social¬≠ist iden¬≠ti¬≠ty through art,‚ÄĚ writes Brinkof. Ide¬≠al¬≠ized rep¬≠re¬≠sen¬≠ta¬≠tions of the rul¬≠ing class hav¬≠ing been aggres¬≠sive¬≠ly scrapped along with the rul¬≠ing class itself, the Bol¬≠she¬≠viks wel¬≠comed any style that could shore up their rev¬≠o¬≠lu¬≠tion¬≠ary cause, total abstrac¬≠tion includ¬≠ed. Alas, though many par¬≠ty offi¬≠cials approved of Tatlin‚Äôs design, they com¬≠mand¬≠ed noth¬≠ing like the resources to build it: ‚ÄúRus¬≠sia would go bank¬≠rupt if it tried to acquire the insane amounts of steel and iron need¬≠ed for the tow¬≠er‚Äôs skele¬≠tal frame¬≠work.‚ÄĚ Per¬≠haps Leon Trot¬≠sky, one of the pro¬≠jec¬≠t‚Äôs dis¬≠senters, was right when he called it ‚Äúimprac¬≠ti¬≠cal and roman¬≠tic‚ÄĚ ‚ÄĒ and per¬≠haps those are the very qual¬≠i¬≠ties that keep Tatlin‚Äôs Tow¬≠er an object of fas¬≠ci¬≠na¬≠tion more than a cen¬≠tu¬≠ry lat¬≠er.
Relat¬≠ed con¬≠tent:
The Utopi¬≠an, Social¬≠ist Designs of Sovi¬≠et Cities
Every¬≠thing You Need to Know About Mod¬≠ern Russ¬≠ian Art in 25 Min¬≠utes: A Visu¬≠al Intro¬≠duc¬≠tion to Futur¬≠ism, Social¬≠ist Real¬≠ism & More
What Makes Kaz¬≠imir Malevich‚Äôs Black Square (1915) Not Just Art, But Impor¬≠tant Art
The Unre¬≠al¬≠ized Projects of Frank Lloyd Wright Get Brought to Life with 3D Dig¬≠i¬≠tal Recon¬≠struc¬≠tions
An Intro¬≠duc¬≠tion to Bru¬≠tal¬≠ism: the Icon¬≠ic Post¬≠war Archi¬≠tec¬≠tur¬≠al Style That Com¬≠bined Utopi¬≠anism and Con¬≠crete
The Futur¬≠ist Archi¬≠tec¬≠tur¬≠al Designs Cre¬≠at¬≠ed by √Čti¬≠enne-Louis Boul¬≠l√©e in the 18th Cen¬≠tu¬≠ry
Based in Seoul,¬†Col¬≠in¬†Marshall¬†writes and broad¬≠casts on cities, lan¬≠guage, and cul¬≠ture. He‚Äôs the author of the newslet¬≠ter¬†Books on Cities¬†as well as the books¬†ŪēúÍĶ≠ žöĒžēĹ ÍłąžßÄ¬†(No Sum¬≠ma¬≠riz¬≠ing Korea) and¬†Kore¬≠an Newtro.¬†Fol¬≠low him on the social net¬≠work for¬≠mer¬≠ly known as Twit¬≠ter at¬†@colinmarshall.

title: Helena Minginowicz Transforms Humble Paper Towel into Ethereal Paintings
description: ‚ÄúCivilizations are remembered through their monuments, but understood through the things they throw away,‚ÄĚ says artist Helena Minginowicz, whose s
body:
‚ÄúCivilizations are remembered through their monuments, but understood through the things they throw away,‚ÄĚ says artist Helena Minginowicz, whose sensitive paintings interrogate our understanding of value. Using airbrushed acrylic, which can be built up in lightweight, translucent layers, the artist takes one of the most quotidian household items as a starting point: paper towel.
With its machine-embossed, moisture-wicking patterns, the absorbent paper comprises an instantly recognizable substrate. The precise, textured flourishes are aesthetically pleasing, and yet it‚Äôs hard to completely separate them from our associations with mass-produced paper products that are designed for one-time use and disposability. This dichotomy sits at he heart of Minginowicz‚Äôs practice, in which she explores ‚Äúhow changing the hierarchy of materials can reshape the way we perceive value, dignity, and the human experience,‚ÄĚ she tells Colossal.

Minginowicz‚Äôs embossed pieces from everyday domestic material are one facet of a broader multimedia approach to materiality in which she creates paintings on canvas and also painstakingly embosses delicate tissues. The paper towel works, in addition to some that are made on supermarket-style plastic bags, are then presented between thick slabs of acrylic, transforming them into objects with substantial heft and dimensionality.
‚ÄúEvery civilization constructs its own hierarchy of values,‚ÄĚ the artist says. ‚ÄúIt decides what deserves to be preserved, admired, and passed on to future generations. Monuments, works of art, symbols, and myths preserve an image of humanity as we wish to remember it‚ÄĒstrong, beautiful, enduring, and heroic. Yet every monument has its reverse.‚ÄĚ
Minginowicz‚Äô imagery draws on the style of Renaissance paintings, especially focusing on expressiveness, intimacy, and the idealized female figure. One might think of Sandro Botticelli‚Äôs ‚ÄúThe Birth of Venus‚ÄĚ or aristocratic portraiture of the era. ‚ÄúFor centuries, painting monumentalized what civilizations wished to remember: saints, heroes, gods, victories, myths, and ideals. I use that same language to ask a different question: Who deserves to be remembered with dignity? Not only heroes. Not only the victorious. But every human being.‚ÄĚ
Minginowicz is currently working toward a solo exhibition at Galerie Prima in Paris, which is slated to open on October 8. Follow updates and see more on Instagram.

schema.headline: Helena Minginowicz Transforms Humble Paper Towel into Ethereal Paintings

title: Uncanny Landscapes in Pen and Ink Span Wooden Panels by John Buck
description: From the Three Patriarchs of Zion Canyon to the swamps of Louisiana to the immense cascade of Niagara Falls, John Buck‚Äôs dreamlike landscapes evok
body:
From the Three Patriarchs of Zion Canyon to the swamps of Louisiana to the immense cascade of Niagara Falls, John Buck‚Äôs dreamlike landscapes evoke the juxtapositions and proportions of dreams. His solo exhibition, Mont Blanc on Wood at Zolla / Lieberman Gallery, draws us to the fuzzy boundary between the familiar and the uncanny.
The Bozeman-based artist is known for his eccentric, often life-size wooden sculptures that draw on folklore, personal memory, and daily observations. Figures are sometimes hybridized with other objects, and idiosyncratic drawings on wood panel reveal expansive landscapes populated by anthropomorphized plants and dramatic rock pinnacles.
‚ÄúLighthouse‚ÄĚ (2024), pen and ink on wood, 27 x 36 ¬Ĺ inches
The works in Mont Blanc on Wood span a range of places and references, emphasizing landscapes where things feel perhaps a little ‚Äúoff.‚ÄĚ The desert stares back in ‚ÄúCactus Grove (No. 13),‚ÄĚ and in ‚ÄúLighthouse (No. 18),‚ÄĚ a light station in the middle of a desert lake shines mostly onto arid, rocky landforms.
‚ÄúBuck reflects on social and political realities, environmental concerns, and the eccentricities of human behavior, all while maintaining a sense of humor and a deep engagement with craft,‚ÄĚ the gallery says. ‚ÄúHis art balances storytelling with formal clarity, inviting viewers into a world where the familiar becomes speculative and symbolic.‚ÄĚ
Mont Blanc on Wood continues through August 8 in Chicago.
‚ÄúCactus Grove‚ÄĚ (2024), pen and ink on wood, 36 ¬Ĺ x 27 inches
‚ÄúThree Patriarchs, Utah‚ÄĚ (2024), pen and ink on wood, 26 ¬Ĺ x 31 ¬Ĺ inches
‚ÄúThe Never Sweat Mine‚ÄĚ (2024), pen and ink on wood, 21 ¬ľ x 26 ¬Ĺ inches
‚ÄúAtchafalaya‚ÄĚ (2024), pen and ink on wood, 26 ¬Ĺ x 32 inches
‚Äú7 Waterfalls (No14),‚ÄĚ (2024), pen and ink on wood, 37 x 27 inches
‚ÄúAspens‚ÄĚ (2024), pen and ink on wood, 26 ¬Ĺ x 37 ¬Ĺ inches
‚ÄúSea Mount‚ÄĚ (2024), pen and ink on wood, 52 x 37 ¬Ĺ inches

title: A People‚Äôs History of Mount Rushmore
description:
Books Newsletter
                Eight art books reframe America 250 and stock photography in the age of AI.
body:
Books Newsletter
                Eight art books reframe America 250 and stock photography in the age of AI.
            
                
                                
                            
                
            
        
            The phrase ‚ÄúAmerica 250‚ÄĚ sends a chill down my spine. But when it comes to confronting the mythology of the United States and narrating its history anew, artists are rising to the challenge, as they always have. With half a year of semiquincentennial fanfare still to come, our editors and critics are turning to books that offer crucial counter-narratives to the nationalism the Trump administration is pushing in arts and culture. Some highlights: the drag queens who descend on New York‚Äôs Fire Island every Fourth of July, a Native history of Six Grandfathers (the site also known as Mount Rushmore), and a catalog for the MONUMENTS exhibition that made waves in Los Angeles last fall.Read our full list below, and let me know what books are reshaping your understanding of US art history. More today, including the political implications of stock photography and a look at John Constable‚Äôs life and art through the seasons.‚ÄĒLakshmi Rivera Amin, associate editor


	
		
			8 Art Books to Reframe America‚Äôs 250th
		
	
	
		The Fourth of July fireworks and barbecues are behind us, but America 250 ‚ÄĒ and all its attendant propaganda ‚ÄĒ is still in full swing. Thankfully, artists, critics, curators, and museum professionals across the nation provide a deep well of books to make sense of this apocalyptic anniversary, using it as an opportunity to uncover ugly truths about United States history that the Trump administration and political right is doing its best to scrub from the record. 
      
      Artist Keisha Scarville‚Äôs series of images meditating on her father‚Äôs passport explores the dimensions of the fraught document, while a photographer chronicles the annual tradition in which drag queens flood Fire Island on the Fourth of July to make their presence known and felt. Matthew Davis retells the story of Mount Rushmore through the desecration of the Black Hills, and contributors to the catalog for the blockbuster MONUMENTS exhibition in Los Angeles consider the layered histories of sculptures and statues across the country. In other words, art reveals new ways of seeing ‚ÄĒ and we can always use more of them.
	
	
		Read More
	

From Our Critics

	
		
			John Constable‚Äôs Four Seasons
		
	
	
		Art historian Susan Owens‚Äôs exquisitely illustrated new book narrates the painter‚Äôs story through his relationship to weather, place, and time. | Lauren Moya Ford
	
	
		Read More
	

Features
ICYMI

	
		
			Every Dog Has Its Artist
		
	
	
		A compassionate new book explores how canine companions across Western art history break down the emotional boundaries between species. | Alisyn Amant
	
	
		Read More
	

From the Archive
                  
                      
                        Books Newsletter

title: A Convicted Antiquities Dealer‚Äôs Web
description:
Daily Newsletter
                Phoenix Ancient Art funneled objects of shaky provenance into dozens of museums. Why are many o
body:
Daily Newsletter
                Phoenix Ancient Art funneled objects of shaky provenance into dozens of museums. Why are many of them still there?
            
                
                                
                            
                
            
        
            In February, the Metropolitan Museum of Art quietly returned a Roman bust to Turkey. It was originally purchased from Phoenix Ancient Art gallery in Geneva, whose founder, Ali Aboutaam, was convicted by a Swiss court of illegally importing antiquities. Why do dozens of other museums still hold the objects they bought from the gallery? Art crime scholar Erin L. Thompson investigates.Also today, artists demand justice for the family of Lorenzo Salgado Araujo, a Houston resident misidentified and murdered by ICE during a traffic stop. Staff Writer Rhea Nayyar writes about the most moving tributes and artworks.‚ÄĒLakshmi Rivera Amin, associate editor


	
		
			Why Do So Many Museums Hold a Convicted Antiquity Dealer‚Äôs Treasures?
		
	
	
		When I went to the Metropolitan Museum of Art a few weeks ago in search of a marble portrait bust of an imperious-looking Roman man, both the sculpture and its pedestal had vanished. Only the label dating the piece to the 2nd century CE remained, marooned on the gallery wall. 

The sculpture had been purchased from Phoenix Ancient Art, a gallery purporting to follow ‚Äúthe antiquities trade‚Äôs most vigorous and stringent procedures of due diligence.‚ÄĚ The gallery touted the effort it put into finding evidence that its artifacts had left their countries of origin long before the enactment of export bans. Equipped with these good provenances, or ownership histories, Phoenix boasted that its antiquities could be ‚Äúcollected in full compliance with all legal and ethical rules.‚ÄĚ  | Erin L. Thompson
	
	
		Read More
	


                    
                    
                    
                        
                            The Bennett Prize Opens Fifth Call for EntriesWomen figurative realist painters can enter to win $75,000 and a traveling solo exhibition. Applications are open through September 19.
                        
                        
                            Learn more
                        
                    
            NewsArtists are memorializing Lorenzo Salgado Araujo, a 52-year-old man who was fatally shot by ICE on July 7, through portraits, illustrations, cartoons, and signs.Manhattan's High Line Art is seeking public feedback on¬†62 proposals¬†for the public artwork commissions that will grace the park‚Äôs plinth in 2029 and 2030.The New York City Department of Health and Mental Hygiene discovered Legionella, the bacterium that causes Legionnaires‚Äô disease, in the Guggenheim Museum‚Äôs cooling tower this week.From Our Critics

From the Archive

                        Hyundai Motor Group ‚Äď The 7th VH AwardA global award for emerging media artists engaging with the context of Asia, featuring grants, a residency with Ars Electronica, and global exhibitions. Read more on¬†Hyperallergic.Deadline: July 21, 2026 |¬†vhaward.com
                    See more in¬†this month‚Äôs¬†list of opportunities¬†for artists, writers, and art workers!¬†

title: Indie Film From the Global Majority: BlackStar Celebrates 15 Years of Cinema for Liberation
description:
Announcement
                The world‚Äôs most significant and groundbreaking filmmakers of color will gather to showcase their work i
body:
Announcement
                The world‚Äôs most significant and groundbreaking filmmakers of color will gather to showcase their work in Philadelphia August 6-9.
            
                
                                
                            
                
            
                
        
            Photo courtesy West Side Familia
    
        
            BlackStar Projects has announced the full schedule of events for the 2026 BlackStar Film Festival, taking place August 6‚Äď9 in Philadelphia and streaming online around the world.¬†Featuring 91 films from over 40 countries worldwide, the lineup includes the North American premiere of Haile Gerima‚Äôs Black Lions‚ÄĒRoman Wolves (2026) on closing night and the world premiere of West Side Familia (2026), from Taylor Hosking, on opening night.The festival is an annual celebration of independent cinema from the global majority and a one-of-a-kind gathering centered on connection, discussion, and learning. Notable guests and speakers at this year‚Äôs festival include Sky Hopinka, Mich√®le Stephenson, Cybil St. Aude-Tate, Felicia Pride, and Alile Sharon Larkin ‚ÄĒ who will be in conversation with Melissa Lyde on August 9, after a retrospective screening of the new 4K restoration of Larkin‚Äôs highly acclaimed 1982 film, A Different Image.¬†Festival HighlightsWest Side Familia ‚ÄĒ Dir. Taylor HoskingOpening Night FilmWest Side Familia follows a legendary Puerto Rican biker crew and community elders on Manhattan‚Äôs Upper West Side as they fight to preserve their neighborhood‚Äôs culture, history and traditions in the face of gentrification and erasure. | World PremiereCall of My Life ‚ÄĒ Dir. Dammy Twitch Photo courtesy Call of My Life, 2025Soluchi, 31, is a radiant and thoughtful call center agent, still carrying the scars of a long relationship with Kalu, a man whose neglect left her questioning her worth. When loneliness threatens to swallow her, a routine customer call connects her to Eli, a magnetic man whose kindness and attention awaken feelings she has long buried.| U.S. Premiere (Festival)¬†An Incomplete Calendar ‚ÄĒ Dir. Sanaz Sohrabi Forgotten songs and archives tell a different story of the developing world‚Äôs political calendar of liberation movements and the fight for oil sovereignty.| North America PremiereThe Store ‚ÄĒ Dir. Fahamu Pecou Photo courtesy The Store, 2026In a forgotten corner of the city, there exists a bodega like no other ‚ÄĒ a gateway to ancestral wisdom and untold possibilities. When patrons step inside looking for something ordinary, a veil between worlds reveals an alternate reality where Black people thrive.| World PremiereTreasure Island (L'√ģle aux tr√©sors) ‚ÄĒ Dir. Miryam CharlesPhoto courtesy Treasure Island, 2026 Summer 1993. Marthe, 12, convinces her brother to run away from home and from the Haitian heritage they both reject. Joined by their cousins in a park, they debate what their future might hold. But as the hours pass, the children begin to realize that what they‚Äôre trying to escape is also what binds them together most deeply. Charles will also participate in a panel discussion on August 7.| World PremiereBlack Lions‚ÄďRoman Wolves ‚ÄĒ Dir. Haile Gerima Photo courtesy Black Lions ‚ÄĒ Roman Wolves, 2026Closing Night FilmHaile Gerima‚Äôs Black Lions ‚ÄĒ Roman Wolves is a five-part epic on Ethiopian resistance to Italy‚Äôs 1935 invasion, revealing a war fought with arms, memory and culture and honoring a nation that refused to kneel.| North America PremiereTo learn more, visit blackstarprojects.org/festival.
                  
                      
                        Announcement

                        BlackStar Film Festival

                        Pennsylvania
